topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The reception hall buzzed with the forced cheer of a hundred well-meaning acquaintances. I clutched my lukewarm glass of champagne, the bubbles doing little to lift the heavy feeling settling in my chest. Then, across the crowded dance floor, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. It was him. My ex. He was laughing, his arm draped casually around a woman who looked effortlessly radiant. A slow, unwelcome warmth began to spread through my limbs, making my hands feel clumsy. I took a deliberate sip of my drink, focusing intently on the condensation beading on the glass, trying to keep my expression neutral. Every time he glanced in my direction, a tightness constricted my throat, and I instinctively shifted my weight, wanting nothing more than to melt into the wallpaper.

[story 2]

I found myself leaning against a pillar near the buffet table, watching the proceedings with a detached sort of fascination. The music swelled, and with it, the unwelcome memory of him surfaced, sharp and unwelcome. When he turned, his eyes swept the room, and for a fleeting second, they locked onto mine. A strange, hollow ache settled low in my stomach. I quickly looked down at my plate, focusing on the arrangement of canapés, a deliberate effort to appear engaged in the mundane. My shoulders seemed to slump further, and I felt a distinct need to disappear into the shadows, to become invisible. The sheer proximity of him, even across the room, made my breathing shallow.

[story 3]

The air in the venue was thick with perfume and celebration. I was trying to navigate the periphery of the gathering, my gaze darting everywhere but the main dance area. It was a futile exercise; every angle seemed to offer a chance encounter. Then, there he was, standing near the entrance, looking impossibly composed. Seeing him sent a wave of inertia through me. My usual brisk pace dissolved; I started moving slower, my steps becoming heavy and uncertain. I managed a strained smile when a mutual friend approached, but the effort cost me. My gaze kept snagging on him, and I could feel a profound weight pressing down on my eyelids, making it difficult to focus on anything but the way he stood there, utterly unaffected.
